planting.note: Direct sow in fall for best germination in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky, sandy or clay-loam, lean soil in full sun; squirreltail is a pioneer grass that germinates most readily in cool weather (fall or early spring), establishing quickly in disturbed, dry soils where other grasses fail. An excellent early colonizer for erosion control on dry, rocky, alkaline sites.

directions: Direct sow in fall for best germination in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky, sandy or clay-loam, lean soil in full sun; squirreltail is a small, cool-season native bunchgrass named for its distinctive seed heads — the very long, spreading, diverging awns (bristles) on each spikelet create a seed head resembling a squirrel's bushy tail. When the seed heads mature and dry, the awns spread outward dramatically, making the plant immediately distinctive and ornamental. One of the most widely distributed native grasses of the arid West — found from the Great Basin and Mojave Desert through the Rocky Mountains and Great Plains on dry, rocky, alkaline soils, often in disturbed areas, rocky road cuts, and desert flats. One of the most important pioneer and restoration grasses for the arid West because it germinates early in cool weather, establishes fast in poor, dry soils, and outcompetes invasive annual grasses (cheatgrass, medusahead) in some conditions. An essential component of Great Basin and Intermountain West restoration seed mixes. The spreading awns act as effective natural seed dispersal mechanisms — attaching to fur, feathers, and clothing.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring in well-drained to moist, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, clay-loam to sandy loam, average soil in full sun; basin wildrye tolerates highly alkaline, saline, and even seasonally wet soils better than most native grasses. An excellent large grass for difficult alkaline or saline situations in the Great Basin and Rocky Mountain West.

directions: Direct sow in fall or early spring in well-drained to moist, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, clay-loam to sandy loam, average soil in full sun; basin wildrye is one of the largest and most robust native bunchgrasses of the Great Basin and intermountain West — a cool-season grass forming massive, upright clumps of blue-gray-green foliage and tall, erect seed stalks rising 4–6 feet tall. The broad, blue-gray, arching leaves are ornamentally striking, resembling 'blue oat grass' but at a dramatically larger scale. Native to sagebrush steppe, salt desert shrubland, and alkaline flats throughout the Great Basin from eastern California to Wyoming and Colorado. Exceptional tolerance of alkaline and saline soils — one of the best native grasses for revegetating saline, alkaline, or disturbed soils in the Great Basin where other grasses struggle. Provides critical winter cover and forage for deer, elk, and pronghorn under deep snow. An outstanding large ornamental grass for bold landscape use in appropriate dry western gardens — the blue-gray foliage and massive clumps create a strong architectural statement. Provides excellent erosion control on steep, dry, alkaline banks.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy or peaty soil in partial shade to full sun; Japanese witch hazel grows naturally in the mountains of Honshu and Kyushu, Japan, where it flowers in late winter on the bare branches before leaves emerge. More heat-tolerant than Chinese witch hazel — a better choice for zones 7-8.

directions: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy or peaty soil in partial shade to full sun; Japanese witch hazel is one of the parents of the widely grown Hamamelis x intermedia hybrid witch hazels (crossed with Chinese witch hazel H. mollis) — producing some of the most widely planted late-winter-flowering shrubs in the world. The species itself produces yellow to yellow-orange, ribbon-like, spidery petals on bare branches in late winter, earlier than most witch hazels, with a pleasant sweet fragrance. More heat-tolerant than the Chinese species, making it a better choice for zone 7–8 gardens in the southeastern US. The outstanding fall color of yellow to orange-red makes it a four-season ornamental shrub. Parent of many named cultivars and interspecific hybrids including 'Arnold Promise' (yellow, very fragrant), 'Jelena' (copper-orange), and 'Diane' (red) — among the most celebrated winter-flowering shrubs in horticulture. Excellent as a woodland garden shrub flowering in the dormant season when nothing else is in bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy soil in partial shade; California spicebush grows naturally along streambanks, canyon bottoms, and moist slopes in the foothills and lower mountains of California, requiring consistent moisture during summer and partial shade in hot, dry climates.

directions: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy soil in partial shade; California spicebush is the western native counterpart of the eastern Carolina allspice (Calycanthus floridus) — a large, suckering deciduous shrub from the stream banks and moist canyon slopes of California's Coast Range and Sierra Nevada foothills. The unusual, dark burgundy-red to brownish-red flowers are unlike those of most ornamental shrubs — large, with many strap-shaped petals in overlapping whorls like a water lily, with a distinctively spicy-fruity fragrance. All parts of the plant have a spicy, slightly vinegar-like fragrance when crushed or cut — the bark was used by Indigenous peoples as a spice and medicine. The large, dark green, sandpaper-textured leaves (rough on the upper surface) are attractive through the growing season. A natural companion to other California riparian and canyon shrubs in mixed planting. More heat-tolerant than the eastern sweetshrub, adapting to hot, dry-summer California coastal and inland valley gardens with summer irrigation.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, sandy or loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; giant wild rye is one of the most shade-tolerant of the large native grasses of the West Coast — growing naturally under oak woodland canopies and in partial shade of chaparral margins along the California coast and foothills.

directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, sandy or loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; giant wild rye is the largest and most dramatic of the native California bunchgrasses — a massive, clump-forming cool-season grass from the coastal bluffs, oak woodlands, and chaparral margins of California and Baja California. Producing broad, blue-gray-green leaves and towering seed stalks 6–8 feet tall in early summer, it creates an impressive, architectural presence in the landscape. One of the few large native grasses tolerating partial shade — growing naturally under the dappled canopy of coast live oak and blue oak woodlands. The cultivar 'Canyon Prince' (a dwarf, silver-blue form selected from Santa Cruz Island) is widely grown in California native plant gardens for its compact size and striking silver-blue foliage color. Extremely drought tolerant once established — summer-dormant in dry-summer California climates. An essential large ornamental grass for California native plant gardens, coastal bluff restorations, and dry-shade situations under oaks where conventional grasses and groundcovers struggle.
